speaker: ALEX
text: Welcome back to Git Going with GitHub. This is episode 45: VS Code Accessibility Deep Dive. I am Alex, and today we are turning VS Code Accessibility Deep Dive from a list of instructions into a working mental model.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And I am Jamie. I will stop us whenever the instructions sound simple on paper but might feel different with a keyboard and screen reader.

speaker: ALEX
text: Keyboard navigation, accessible views, terminal access, signals, speech, and Copilot accessibility in VS Code. That is the surface description. Underneath it, we are building judgment: where to focus, what to ignore, and how to verify the result.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So we are not using the audio as a shortcut around learning. We are using it to make the learning easier to enter.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. A good audio lesson gives someone enough context to try the work with confidence, even before they open the written material.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Okay, set the room for us. What are we walking into?

speaker: ALEX
text: Accessibility Features for Power Users: Challenge 10: Go Local depends on the accessibility features covered in this chapter. The next useful detail is concrete: Configure these settings before your first local commit.

speaker: ALEX
text: The next layer is this. Here is the learner-facing version. Mac users: Substitute Cmd for Ctrl and Option for Alt in all shortcuts below. A good GitHub workflow is like a well-run meeting: everyone knows the topic, the next action, and who has the floor.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What is the one idea that makes the next few steps less mysterious?

speaker: ALEX
text: This is the move inside Find in Current File (Ctrl+F): when the Find widget opens, three toggle buttons refine what matches.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That feels much more doable when you say it as one move.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. The magic is not speed. The magic is knowing what changed and why it matters.

speaker: ALEX
text: Now bring the learner back to the room. Anchor this part on Screen reader interactions inside the Find widget. Replace (Ctrl+H): Opens the Find widget with a second input for the replacement text.

speaker: ALEX
text: For a learner, the useful signals are concrete. Toggles are announced as checkboxes - press Space to toggle each one. Match count is announced as you type (example: 3 of 12 matches). F3 / Shift+F3 move through matches while the widget stays open. Escape closes the widget and returns focus to your last cursor position. Ctrl+Shift+1 - replace the current match. Ctrl+Alt+Enter - replace all matches at once.

speaker: ALEX
text: A solid navigation habit is to prove where you are before activating controls. Headings, landmarks, and the address bar are not trivia; they are your map.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What would you say to someone who is already bracing for this to be too much?

speaker: ALEX
text: The reason this matters is simple: the global Search panel has a rich filtering system - all keyboard-accessible.

speaker: ALEX
text: The useful habit is simple: orient, act, verify, then continue. That pause between action and trust is part of the work.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Give me the sequence, because order matters here.

speaker: ALEX
text: Do not treat Type-to-Filter in Tree Views as decoration. In the Explorer file tree and the Source Control changes list, type characters to narrow visible items. The workshop is closer to rehearsal than lecture. You hear the move, try the move, and then check what changed.

speaker: ALEX
text: First, focus the Explorer (Ctrl+Shift+E). Then, start typing a filename - a filter input appears at the bottom of the tree. After that, the tree instantly narrows to matching files. Finally, press Escape to clear the filter and restore full view. That small check between steps is what makes the workflow reliable.

speaker: JAMIE
text: If I am listening before the workshop starts, what should settle in my mind first?

speaker: ALEX
text: If the interface shifts, Go to Symbol with Inline Filtering (Ctrl+Shift+O) is still useful because in any Markdown file, Ctrl+Shift+O opens a symbol picker populated by every heading. For someone navigating by keyboard or screen reader, this detail matters: Type to narrow the list, then press Enter to jump.

speaker: ALEX
text: This is where the talk moves from concept to action. Learning Cards: Keyboard Navigation and Find has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: The room should hear these as checkpoints. Press Ctrl+M to toggle Tab focus mode -- when on, Tab moves focus between UI elements instead of inserting a tab character. Use Ctrl+G then type a line number to jump directly to any line; type 10:5 to land at line 10, column 5. In the Find widget (Ctrl+F), match count is announced as you type (e.g., "3 of 12 matches"); press F3 / Shift+F3 to step through results. Keyboard Shortcuts editor (Ctrl+K Ctrl+S): After typing a search query, your screen reader announces "Use Ctrl+Down Arrow to access the searched shortcut details" -- press Ctrl+Down to jump from the search input directly to the matching results table. Disable. Press Alt+Z to toggle word wrap so long lines stay visible without horizontal scrolling at high zoom. Increase font size with Ctrl+= (Mac: Cmd+=) independently of your OS magnification for sharper text rendering.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Now it sounds like a workflow instead of a wall of instructions.

speaker: ALEX
text: That is where confidence comes from: not from never getting lost, but from knowing how to recover.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Where do you want a learner to place their attention here?

speaker: ALEX
text: The teaching point here is not the label; it is the move. The Problems panel (Ctrl+Shift+M) shows all errors, warnings, and informational messages from linters, compilers, and extensions for every open file in your workspace.

speaker: ALEX
text: A solid pull request habit is to separate three questions: what changed, why it changed, and what still needs review. That keeps the conversation useful instead of noisy.

speaker: ALEX
text: Before the learner moves on. Opening the Problems Panel has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: That becomes easier when you listen for these cues. Keyboard: Ctrl+Shift+M. Status Bar: Click the errors/warnings count (bottom-left of window). Menu Bar: View then Problems. From the editor: Press F8 to jump to the next problem (cycles through errors in the current file).

speaker: JAMIE
text: Give me the version that sounds like an instructor, not a manual.

speaker: ALEX
text: Understanding Problem Entries has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: If someone is taking notes, this is the short list. Severity icon: Error (red circle with X), Warning (yellow triangle), Info (blue circle with i). Message: Description of the problem. Source: Which tool reported it (e.g., "markdownlint", "eslint", "Pylance"). File and line: Where the problem is located.

speaker: ALEX
text: Now slow down for the part people usually miss. Learning Cards: Problems Panel has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the part that makes the next action easier. Press Ctrl+Shift+M to focus the Problems panel. Your screen reader announces the total count. Each problem is read as: severity, message, source, file name, and line number. Press Enter on any problem to jump directly to that line in the editor. Use F8 / Shift+F8 from inside the editor to cycle through problems without opening the panel. The status bar errors/warnings count updates in real time and is announced when you Tab to it. Problems are color-coded: red for errors, yellow for warnings, blue for info.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on 14. The Terminal. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: This is the move inside 14. The Terminal: VS Code includes a fully featured integrated terminal. That matters in practice: You can run shell commands, Git operations, and scripts without leaving the editor.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That is the kind of detail that keeps a screen reader user oriented.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. The named thing - the heading, tab, field, branch, or button - is the handhold.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the moment where the page starts to make sense. Anchor this part on Terminal Shell Integration. VS Code's shell integration enhances the terminal. This is the part to say slowly: Enable Terminal IntelliSense: Settings (Ctrl+,) then search terminal.integrated.suggest.enabled then set to on. The durable skill is not memorizing one screen. It is knowing how to find your footing when the screen changes.

speaker: ALEX
text: Listen for the small confirmations in this list. Command decoration marks - visual indicators showing where each command started and whether it succeeded or failed. Run recent command (Ctrl+R in terminal) - VS Code's quick pick of your recent commands, searchable by name. Terminal IntelliSense (Ctrl+Space) - completion suggestions for shell commands, file paths, and arguments.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Learning Cards: Terminal. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Learning Cards: Terminal has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: The useful version is not abstract; it sounds like this. Press Ctrl+ ` to toggle the terminal. Your screen reader announces "Terminal" and the shell prompt. The terminal acts like a standard text input - type commands and press Enter. Press Alt+H while in the terminal for a full list of terminal-specific keyboard shortcuts. Use Ctrl+R to open the "Run Recent Command" picker - a searchable list of your recent commands. Terminal Navigation Mode: Commands for moving between lines help when reviewing output with a screen reader. VS Code enforces a minimum contrast ratio (4.5:1 by default) for terminal text.

speaker: ALEX
text: The next point gives the learner a handle. Do not treat 15. Copilot Chat Window as decoration. The Copilot Chat window (Ctrl+Alt+I, or Chat: Open Chat from the Command Palette if your keymap differs) is your conversational AI assistant within VS Code. That is not trivia. It can answer questions, generate code, explain code, fix problems, and help with documentation.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Chat Modes. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: If the interface shifts, Chat Modes is still useful because switch modes using the mode picker at the top of the Chat view, or use keyboard shortcuts.

speaker: ALEX
text: A few details make that real. workbench.action.chat.openAsk - Ask mode. workbench.action.chat.openEdit - Edit mode. workbench.action.chat.openAgent - Agent mode.

speaker: ALEX
text: Hold that next to this. Put Using Chat Participants into plain language. Type @ in the chat input to see available participants. The interface gets easier when it becomes a set of named places instead of a wall of controls.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here are the anchors worth keeping. @workspace - Ask questions about your entire codebase. @vscode - Ask about VS Code settings and features. @terminal - Run commands or explain terminal output.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So the learner is not behind if they stop there and check the page.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Pausing to verify is not a detour; it is how you keep control of the workflow.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Learning Cards: Copilot Chat. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Learning Cards: Copilot Chat has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: That shows up in the workshop in a few specific ways. Press Ctrl+Alt+I, or run Chat: Open Chat from the Command Palette, to open Chat. Focus lands in the text input - start typing your question. After submitting, wait for the response to complete (audio cue plays if accessibility.signals.chatResponseReceived is on). Press Alt+F2 (Accessible View) to read the complete response in a clean, navigable text view. Navigate response content with Up/Down Arrow in the Accessible View. Press Escape to return to the chat input for follow-up questions. The Chat view appears in the sidebar and respects your zoom level and font settings.

speaker: ALEX
text: That connects to another useful point. This part earns its place because when Copilot's Agent mode is running a terminal command and needs your input -- such as a password prompt, confirmation, or interactive installer question -- VS Code displays a question carousel in the Chat panel. That gives the learner a foothold: the carousel shows what the terminal is asking and lets you respond without switching focus to the terminal manually.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What do you want them to do when the plan breaks?

speaker: ALEX
text: 16. Accessible Help, Accessible View, and Accessible Diff: See also: Appendix G: VS Code Reference has a complete keyboard shortcut reference for all VS Code accessibility features. The next useful detail is concrete: VS Code has a family of purpose-built accessibility features that give screen reader users complete, structured access to content that is otherwise conveyed visually or through dynamic regions.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the practical turn. Here is the learner-facing version. Every interactive area of VS Code - the editor, the terminal, the diff view, the Copilot Chat panel - has its own keyboard commands. Put another way, accessible Help surfaces those commands in a plain-text, fully readable dialog, tailored to exactly where your focus is right now. A good GitHub workflow is like a well-run meeting: everyone knows the topic, the next action, and who has the floor.

speaker: JAMIE
text: How should someone ask for help in a way that gets them unstuck faster?

speaker: ALEX
text: This is the move inside How to open Accessible Help: the dialog is announced with a heading and a complete list of keyboard shortcuts for that specific widget. That matters in practice: Press Escape to dismiss and return focus to where you were.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That is a useful checkpoint before anyone starts pressing keys.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Checkpoints turn uncertainty into information.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What should happen before anyone copies and runs it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Anchor this part on Example output when pressing Alt+H in the editor. Use Accessible Help as your first action whenever you land somewhere new in VS Code.

speaker: ALEX
text: Treat examples as spoken recipes, not decorations. You may hear something like Accessible Help: Editor; Press F8 to jump to the next error or warning.; Press Shift+F8 to jump to the previous error or warning.; Press Ctrl+Shift+M to open the Problems panel.; Press F12 to go to a definition.; Press Alt+F12 to peek a definition inline. Read the command, understand what it changes, then run it only when the repository state matches the lesson.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on 16.2 Accessible View - Reading Dynamic and Streamed Content. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: The reason this matters is simple: accessible View (Alt+F2) gives screen reader users a clean, static, fully readable version of content that is otherwise presented dynamically, in tooltips, or in streaming form.

speaker: JAMIE
text: How would you walk the room through that step by step?

speaker: ALEX
text: Recommended workflow for Copilot Chat has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: First, type your prompt in the Chat input. Then, wait for the response to finish (NVDA: live region announcements stop; JAWS: typing indicator disappears; VoiceOver: busy state clears). After that, press Alt+F2 - Accessible View opens with the complete response. Finally, navigate with Up/Down Arrow through the response. The point is not speed; the point is never losing your place.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What does the learner do first, second, and then after that?

speaker: ALEX
text: Start here: Press Escape to return to the chat input. Each step should leave a trace you can name.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Turn that into a path someone can follow.

speaker: ALEX
text: Recommended workflow for hover documentation has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Start here: Navigate to a symbol or link with keyboard. Then: Press Ctrl+K I to trigger hover programmatically (no mouse needed). Next: Press Alt+F2 to open Accessible View with the full hover content. Last: Press Escape to dismiss. Each step should leave a trace you can name.

speaker: ALEX
text: This is the part worth saying out loud. Put 16.3 Accessible Diff Viewer - Reading Changes Without Visual Scanning into plain language. When you open a file diff - in Source Control, in the GitHub PR extension, or during a merge conflict - VS Code normally shows it as a side-by-side or inline visual view. The useful version is: For screen reader users, tracking which lines changed and how can be difficult without a structured reading mode.

speaker: JAMIE
text: I like that because it gives people permission to slow down.

speaker: ALEX
text: That is the goal. We want the page to feel explorable, not fragile.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What should they understand before typing anything?

speaker: ALEX
text: The teaching point here is not the label; it is the move. For each hunk (a block of related changes), the viewer announces. That is the difference between guessing and knowing: This gives you the complete picture of what changed, in reading order, without visual diff scanning.

speaker: ALEX
text: These are the pieces that turn the idea into a usable move. The hunk number and total hunk count (Hunk 2 of 5). The line range affected. Each line, prefixed with its change type.

speaker: ALEX
text: Treat examples as spoken recipes, not decorations. You may hear something like Hunk 1 of 3 - lines 12 to 18; Unchanged: Screen Reader Cheat Sheet; - Line removed: Quick reference for NVDA users.; + Line added: Quick reference for NVDA, JAWS, and VoiceOver users.; Unchanged:; Unchanged: Use this document during the workshop. Read the command, understand what it changes, then run it only when the repository state matches the lesson.

speaker: ALEX
text: Put that beside the next piece. Practical uses during this workshop has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: This is where the lesson becomes something you can check. Before approving a PR: Open the diff then F7 to enter the first hunk then navigate each change then F7 for next hunk then repeat until all hunks reviewed. During a merge conflict: The conflict markers ( ) appear as lines in the viewer - you can read both conflicting versions before deciding which to keep. After Copilot generates an edit: Open the diff (Ctrl+Shift+G then navigate to the changed file then Enter) then review exactly what Copilot changed vs. what was there before.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Audio cues for diffs. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Audio cues for diffs: With accessibility.signals.diffLineInserted and accessibility.signals.diffLineDeleted both set to on in Settings, VS Code plays a distinct tone when your cursor moves over an added line (higher pitched) or a removed line (lower pitched). The next useful detail is concrete: You receive change-type information through sound before the line text is announced.

speaker: ALEX
text: Now shift from knowing the term to using it. Learning Cards: Accessible Help, View, and Diff has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: The parts worth keeping in working memory are these. Press Alt+H in any VS Code panel to open Accessible Help -- it lists every keyboard shortcut available in your current context. Press Alt+F2 to open Accessible View for Copilot responses, hover documentation, or terminal output -- read the full content with arrow keys. In a diff view, press F7 to enter the Accessible Diff Viewer and step through changes hunk by hunk with line-level change-type announcements. Accessible View (Alt+F2) renders Copilot responses as plain text in a scrollable pane -- easier to read at high zoom than the streaming chat panel. In diff views, enable accessibility.signals.diffLineInserted for an audible cue on added lines so you do not rely solely on color. The Accessible Diff Viewer text uses the same font size as your editor -- zoom settings apply automatically.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on 17. Accessibility Signals. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: This is the move inside 17. Accessibility Signals: VS Code communicates editor state through Accessibility Signals -- non-verbal cues that tell you what is happening as you move through code, run commands, and interact with Copilot. That matters in practice: Signals replaced the older "Audio Cues" system (deprecated since VS Code 1.85) and are significantly more powerful.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That is the part I would want someone to say out loud while they work.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. A learner should always know what they are trying to prove before they take the next action.

speaker: ALEX
text: This is where confidence starts to build. Anchor this part on How Signals Work: The Dual-Channel Architecture. Every accessibility signal has two independent channels that you control separately. This is the part to say slowly: Each channel accepts one of these values. The durable skill is not memorizing one screen. It is knowing how to find your footing when the screen changes.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What should someone listen for when a lesson offers more than one tool path?

speaker: ALEX
text: The reason this matters is simple: VS Code provides two commands that let you browse every available signal, hear what each one sounds like, and toggle them on or off without editing settings.json. The listener should be able to check this: These are the fastest way to configure signals.

speaker: ALEX
text: That matters because of the next idea. Do not treat Volume Control as decoration. Control signal volume independently from your system volume. That is not trivia. Set this in Settings (Ctrl+,) by searching "signal volume" or add it to settings.json.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Complete Signal Reference. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: If the interface shifts, Complete Signal Reference is still useful because VS Code registers 30+ accessibility signals organized into categories. For someone navigating by keyboard or screen reader, this detail matters: The tables below list every signal, its setting key, the sound it plays, and whether it supports announcements.

speaker: ALEX
text: Keep the thread going. Put Editor Signals into plain language. These fire when your cursor moves to a line or position with a specific marker. The useful version is: Line vs Position signals: The lineHasError signal fires once when your cursor enters the line. The interface gets easier when it becomes a set of named places instead of a wall of controls.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So this is less about memorizing and more about noticing.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. Once the learner can name the move, the interface becomes much less intimidating.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Diff Signals. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: The teaching point here is not the label; it is the move. These fire when navigating changes in the diff editor or reviewing pull requests. That is the difference between guessing and knowing: These are critical for pull request review.

speaker: ALEX
text: Another way to ground it. This part earns its place because enable taskCompleted and taskFailed immediately. That gives the learner a foothold: when you run git push or npm test in the terminal, you hear whether it succeeded without switching back to the terminal panel.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Chat and Copilot Signals. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Chat and Copilot Signals: chatResponseReceived plays a randomly chosen variant each time (responseReceived1 through responseReceived4). The next useful detail is concrete: This prevents habituation -- your brain stays alert to the signal instead of filtering it out after hearing the same sound repeatedly.

speaker: ALEX
text: Keep the teaching thread moving. Here is the learner-facing version. These fire on user-triggered actions and use the "userGesture" value to distinguish manual saves from auto-saves. Put another way, set these to "userGesture" rather than "on" if auto-save is enabled, to avoid a sound on every keystroke pause. A good GitHub workflow is like a well-run meeting: everyone knows the topic, the next action, and who has the floor.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Debounce Settings for Position Signals. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: This is the move inside Debounce Settings for Position Signals: when you hold an arrow key and your cursor moves rapidly through lines, position-based signals (error/warning at position) could fire dozens of times per second. That matters in practice: VS Code debounces these by default.

speaker: ALEX
text: Keep the teaching thread moving. Anchor this part on Recommended Workshop Profile. Add this to your VS Code settings.json (Ctrl+Shift+P then "Preferences: Open User Settings (JSON)"). This is the part to say slowly: This profile enables core sounds for everyone and sets announcements to auto so they activate only when a screen reader is detected.

speaker: ALEX
text: On the ground, that means a few things. lineHasError -- "Error on Line" as you navigate code. taskCompleted / taskFailed -- know when git operations finish. chatResponseReceived -- know when Copilot is done responding. lineHasBreakpoint -- confirm breakpoint placement during debugging.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Migrating from Legacy Audio Cues. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: The reason this matters is simple: if you previously configured the older audioCues. The listener should be able to check this: settings (deprecated since VS Code 1.85), VS Code automatically maps them to the new accessibility.signals.

speaker: ALEX
text: Keep the teaching thread moving. Learning Cards: Accessibility Signals has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is what that changes in practice. Run Help: List Signal Sounds from the Command Palette (Ctrl+Shift+P) to preview every available signal tone and decide which to enable. Enable accessibility.signals.lineHasError so you hear an immediate tone when your cursor lands on a line with an error -- no need to open the Problems panel. Set signals to "announcement" channel if you prefer spoken labels (e.g., "error on line") over tones. Signals provide a second channel of awareness -- hearing a "task completed" chime means you can keep your magnifier focused on your editor instead of watching the terminal. Enable accessibility.signals.chatResponseReceived to get an audible notification when Copilot finishes generating a response. Pair error signals with High Contrast themes so both color and sound reinforce error locations.

speaker: JAMIE
text: How do we make tool choice feel like access, not pressure?

speaker: ALEX
text: If the interface shifts, 18. VS Code Speech - Voice Input and Output is still useful because the VS Code Speech extension adds speech-to-text and text-to-speech capabilities to VS Code. For someone navigating by keyboard or screen reader, this detail matters: All voice processing happens locally on your machine - no audio data is sent to any online service.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What is the ordered workflow?

speaker: ALEX
text: Put Installing VS Code Speech into plain language. After installation, a microphone icon appears in all Chat input fields and new voice commands become available in the Command Palette. The useful version is: Microphone permissions: On macOS, go to System Settings, Privacy and Security, Microphone, and confirm Visual Studio Code is enabled.

speaker: ALEX
text: Walk it in order: Open Extensions: Ctrl+Shift+X (Mac: Cmd+Shift+X); Search for VS Code Speech; Find VS Code Speech (publisher: Microsoft, identifier: ms-vscode.vscode-speech); and Press Enter to open the extension detail page, then Tab to "Install" and press Enter. Keep it that plain: know where you are, make the move, check the result.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Editor Dictation - Type with Your Voice. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: The teaching point here is not the label; it is the move. Editor dictation lets you speak and have your words appear as text wherever your cursor is. That is the difference between guessing and knowing: This works in the code editor, the SCM commit input box, and the comments field when reviewing pull requests.

speaker: ALEX
text: These are the details that keep the idea from floating away. When dictation starts, the accessibility signal voiceRecordingStarted plays (if configured in section 17). Your screen reader may also announce "Recording started.". Dictated text appears at the cursor position and is announced by your screen reader as it is inserted, just like typed text. Press Escape to stop. The voiceRecordingStopped signal plays. If you do not hear dictated text being announced, check that your screen reader is in focus mode (NVDA: Insert+Space to toggle) so it reads editor changes.

speaker: ALEX
text: Keep the teaching thread moving. This part earns its place because instead of typing prompts, you can speak them. That gives the learner a foothold: this works in the Chat panel, inline chat, and quick chat. That is the difference between following directions and owning the workflow.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the part to remember. Ctrl+I with Speech installed starts voice input. If the Chat view is not focused, it opens the Chat view. If you are in the editor, it opens inline chat. Speak your prompt instead of typing. Your screen reader announces the transcribed text as it appears in the input field. When the response arrives, press Alt+F2 (Accessible View) to read it at your own pace, just as you would with a typed prompt. The automatic submission after a pause may catch you off guard. If you need more time to compose a multi-sentence prompt, set accessibility.voice.speechTimeout to 0 and submit manually with Ctrl+Enter.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Text-to-Speech - Listen to Chat Responses. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Text-to-Speech - Listen to Chat Responses: VS Code Speech can read Copilot Chat responses aloud. The next useful detail is concrete: Each chat response shows a speaker icon you can activate to hear that specific response.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Automatic read-aloud after voice input. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the learner-facing version. Enable this setting to have every Chat response automatically spoken aloud when you used voice to ask the question.

speaker: ALEX
text: Start here: You speak a question using voice chat. Then: Copilot responds in text. Next: The response is automatically read aloud. Last: To stop playback mid-sentence, press Escape or activate the stop icon. If one step does not match what you hear, stop there and re-orient.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Manual read-aloud for any response. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: This is the move inside Manual read-aloud for any response: even without autoSynthesize, every Chat response has a speaker icon. That matters in practice: Activate it to hear that specific response read aloud.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the part to remember. Text-to-speech uses a separate audio channel from your screen reader. Both may speak at the same time, which can be confusing. Recommended approach: If you use a screen reader, you may prefer to keep autoSynthesize off and use Accessible View (Alt+F2) to read responses yourself. The text-to-speech voice is more useful for sighted users who want a hands-free experience. If you do want to try text-to-speech alongside your screen reader, reduce your screen reader volume or temporarily mute it while Copilot speaks.

speaker: ALEX
text: Keep the teaching thread moving. Anchor this part on "Hey Code" - Hands-Free Activation. You can configure VS Code to listen continuously for the wake phrase "Hey Code" to start a voice chat session without touching the keyboard. This is the part to say slowly: When "Hey Code" listening is active, a microphone icon appears in the status bar to show that VS Code is listening for the wake phrase. The durable skill is not memorizing one screen. It is knowing how to find your footing when the screen changes.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Language Configuration. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: The reason this matters is simple: VS Code Speech supports 26 languages. The listener should be able to check this: By default it matches your VS Code display language.

speaker: JAMIE
text: How do you keep commands from becoming magic words?

speaker: ALEX
text: Do not treat Custom Keybindings for Voice as decoration. You can assign your own shortcuts for voice commands. That is not trivia. Open the Keyboard Shortcuts editor (Ctrl+K Ctrl+S) and search for "voice" or "dictation".

speaker: ALEX
text: Treat examples as spoken recipes, not decorations. You may hear something like [; {; "key": "ctrl+u",; "command": "workbench.action.chat.startVoiceChat",; "when": "!voiceChatInProgress"; },; {; "key": "ctrl+u",; "command": "workbench.action.chat.stopListeningAndSubmit",; "when": "voiceChatInProgress"; },; {; "key": "ctrl+d",; "command". Read the command, understand what it changes, then run it only when the repository state matches the lesson.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Supported Platforms. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: If the interface shifts, Supported Platforms is still useful because on Linux, the extension requires the ALSA shared library (libasound). For someone navigating by keyboard or screen reader, this detail matters: Install it with sudo apt install libasound2 on Debian/Ubuntu if it is not already present.

speaker: ALEX
text: Keep the teaching thread moving. Put 19. Markdown Authoring in VS Code into plain language. You write Markdown in nearly every challenge of this workshop -- issue descriptions, pull request bodies, README updates, and agent files. The useful version is: VS Code has built-in tools and extensions that make Markdown authoring faster, catch formatting mistakes before you commit, and work well with screen readers and keyboard navigation. The interface gets easier when it becomes a set of named places instead of a wall of controls.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Markdown Preview. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: The teaching point here is not the label; it is the move. VS Code includes a live Markdown preview so you can see rendered output alongside your source. That is the difference between guessing and knowing: The preview pane renders HTML, so your screen reader announces headings, links, and list items using their semantic roles.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the part to remember. Side-by-side preview: Press Ctrl+K V (Mac: Cmd+K V) to open a preview pane to the right of your editor. As you type, the preview updates automatically. Full preview: Press Ctrl+Shift+V (Mac: Cmd+Shift+V) to replace the editor tab with a rendered preview. Press the same shortcut again to return to the source. Scroll sync: The preview scrolls in sync with the editor by default. If you scroll in the source, the preview follows.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on The markdownlint Extension. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: This part earns its place because the markdownlint extension (identifier: DavidAnson.vscode-markdownlint) catches common Markdown mistakes as you type, the same way a spell checker catches typos. That gives the learner a foothold: once installed, problems appear as wavy underlines in the editor and as entries in the Problems panel (Ctrl+Shift+M).

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the part to remember. Heading levels that skip (jumping from to ). Missing blank lines before and after headings, lists, and code blocks. Inconsistent list markers (mixing - and ). Trailing spaces and hard tabs.

speaker: ALEX
text: The path is straightforward once it is named. Step one is open Extensions: Ctrl+Shift+X (Mac: Cmd+Shift+X). Step two is search for markdownlint. Step three is install the one by David Anson. If one step does not match what you hear, stop there and re-orient.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Outline View for Headings. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Outline View for Headings: The Outline view shows the heading structure of your Markdown file as a navigable tree -- think of it as a table of contents you can jump through. The next useful detail is concrete: The Go to Symbol list (Ctrl+Shift+O) announces each heading with its level -- for example, "H2 Installation" or "H3 Configuring rules." This is the fastest way to verify your document structure without scrolling through the entire file.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the part to remember. Open Outline: Press Ctrl+Shift+O (Mac: Cmd+Shift+O) to open the Go to Symbol quick-pick, which lists every heading in the file. Type to filter, then press Enter to jump. Outline panel: The Outline view also appears in the Explorer sidebar. Press Ctrl+Shift+E to open Explorer, then Tab until you reach the Outline section. Breadcrumbs: The breadcrumb bar at the top of the editor shows your current heading context. Press Ctrl+Shift+. to focus breadcrumbs and navigate between headings.

speaker: ALEX
text: Keep the teaching thread moving. Here is the learner-facing version. If you have GitHub Copilot enabled (see Chapter 16), it can help with Markdown authoring directly in the editor. Put another way, these features save time when you are writing issue descriptions or pull request bodies during the workshop challenges. A good GitHub workflow is like a well-run meeting: everyone knows the topic, the next action, and who has the floor.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the part to remember. Generate tables: Type a comment like and Copilot suggests a formatted Markdown table. Press Tab to accept. Fix formatting: Select a block of text, open inline chat (Ctrl+I), and type "fix the Markdown formatting." Copilot restructures headings, adds missing blank lines, and corrects list indentation. Suggest alt text: Select an image link like, open inline chat, and ask "suggest alt text for this image." Copilot proposes a description based on the filename and surrounding context. Complete link syntax: Start typing [link text]( and Copilot often autocompletes the URL from your recent files or repository structure.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What should feel predictable before the first live session starts?

speaker: ALEX
text: This is the move inside Why This Matters for the Workshop: students write Markdown in every single challenge -- from the very first issue you file in Chapter 5 to the agent file you create in Chapter 20. That matters in practice: Markdown is also how you write pull request descriptions (Chapter 6) and README files.

speaker: ALEX
text: Keep the teaching thread moving. Anchor this part on If You Get Stuck. Next: Chapter 13: How Git Works Back: Chapter 11: VS Code Interface Related appendices: Appendix G: VS Code Reference.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What should people carry with them after this?

speaker: ALEX
text: Carry the map. Know what page or tool you are in, know which action you are taking, and know what confirmation should follow. If the confirmation is missing, pause. That pause is not wasted time; it is professional judgment.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That is a better way to say it than just follow the steps.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. Steps matter, but understanding wins. That is episode 45. Next in the series is episode 46, where we keep building the same contributor muscles.
